w:r.w:t: from all phrases that currently appears as default
phrase should be generated next to the ‘… appear in reasonable condition

report and if No is selected, ‘No repair is currently needed’, is omitted.
Where visible these appear in reasonable condition to type and age. No repair is currently
Where visible these appear in reasonable condition to type and age.
Condition Rating: 1/2/3 should be the last input selection
just like you have done in the report
This is missing in several section
There should be one line spacing between the paragraphs, currently, there is none which is why the paragraphs do not look fully separated.
The outer faces of the chimney stack
(Wrong place - has missing sections- this is s repair phrase)
The outer faces of the chimney stack
Add this to the paragraph above -
The chimney stacks are installed on the centre, front and side of the building.
Add this to the paragraph above -
Where visible these appear in reasonable condition to type and age. No repair is currently
needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
Add an option to each section that as ‘Defect repair required’ Yes/No. When user select Yes,
, is added to the report and if No is selected, ‘
aerial attached to the chimney is loose. This should be repaired soon.
satellite dish attached to the chimney is loose and rusted. This should be repaired soon.
The following last standard text for the section is missing –

The chimney stack(s) should regularly be maintained to prevent water penetration and to keep the stacks in good condition. The condition of the chimney pots, flaunching and flashing should be checked during normal maintenance routine to ensure water tightness of the stacks.
It was more difficult to detect leakages to roof covering because it was not raining at the time of my inspection. However, no signs were present to suggest that the roof may be leaking.
Where did you lift the following phrase from?
The inspection of the roof covering was undertaken in dry weather conditions. It should be
noted that certain defects, particularly those relating to water penetration, are more readily
identifiable during or following periods of rainfall.
(this is better replacement for the above)
visual examination was carried out from ground level and from all accessible vantage points. No
evidence of active water ingress, staining to ceilings or upper walls, or other indicators of roof
leakage were observed at the time of inspection.
(parts of this phrase requires that the use has selected ‘no defects found or no repairs required
The pitched roof structure over the main building, back addition and dormer window are
covered with original, natural and mineral felt tiles and sheets.
(This paragraph is not complete and is missing the surveyor’s remarks)
Where visible these appear in reasonable condition to type and age. No repair is currently
needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
This above problem is repeated with the ‘flat roof report below, also, the report should have been generated in reverse order – flat roof comment first and followed by flat roof felt comment.
Flat Roof Felt: Flat felt roof coverings have a comparatively short life and will require regular
maintenance checks. Even though the general condition of the felt may appear satisfactory to
type and age, repairs or replacement must be expected in the future. It is difficult to predict
when this will be required as felt roofs can breakdown with little warning, even when visually
The flat roof structure over the back addition and bay window are covered with replacement and
(This should be generated first before felt comment).
All the below have been generated as separate paragraphs but should have been two paragraph
The mansard roof structure over the main building is covered with original sheets.
Where visible these appear in reasonable condition. No repair is currently needed. The property
must be maintained in the normal way.
Flashing: The waterproofing at the junction of the roof covering and wall (called the flashing),
appears to be formed in lead, mortar and tiles.
These appear in reasonable condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be
The mansard roof structure over the main building is covered with original sheets.
Where visible these appear in reasonable condition. No repair is currently needed. The property
must be maintained in the normal way.

appears to be formed in lead, mortar and tiles.
These appear in reasonable condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be
The above problem is repeated throughout the report and needs to be corrected so that the report will appear correct and read better.
The below should be one paragraph:
The deflection to the front, side and rear roof slope(s) is likely to be caused by damaged roof
You should contact a qualified person to replace damaged timber or strengthened any
weakened or small timber so that the roof structure can adequately bear the weight of the roof
The deflection to the front, side and rear roof slope(s) is likely to be caused by damaged roof
You should contact a qualified person to replace damaged timber or strengthened any
weakened or small timber so that the roof structure can adequately bear the weight of the roof
The below should be one paragraph:
The surface of the roof slope to the main building and back addition is uneven or undulating.
This is normal for this type of roof and is no cause for concern. No repair is currently needed.
The property must be maintained in the normal way. The timber used in older roofs can
gradually change shape or distort during normal use. This is not a problem and is normal for
The comment below requires an input
Roof adequate: Yes/No – Sagging Roof Yes/No – Defects Noted Yes/No, Significant work required Yes/No –
the sentence will be generated according to the selection criteria.
The roof structure appears to be adequately performing its functions, and there is no sagging,
or any defects noted, and no significant repair works are necessary.
Because this report has covered data capture for flats, the
should have been generated as default as the end of this section:
below should have been generated because
Roof was reported on in the generated report.
You should ask the management company whether any repairs have been undertaken in the past or proposed for the roof covering.

Your Legal Advisers should confirm responsibility of repairs to the flat roof covering from the management company and that there is an adequate annual sinking fund set aside for repairs and/or replacement should the need arise, so that unexpected repair bill or increased service charge be avoided.
The below should be one paragraph:
Where visible at ground level, the rainwater goods are made up mainly of plastic, cast iron,
asbestos cement, concrete and metal gutters and downpipes.
These are in reasonable condition to type and age. No repair is currently needed. The property
must be maintained in the normal way.
The rainwater gutters and pipes are shared with a neighbouring property or properties. You
should check with your legal adviser about your rights and obligations (See Section I3).
The property must be maintained in the normal way.
Because this report has covered data capture for flats, the following should have been generated as default as the end of this section:
You should ask the management company whether any repairs have been undertaken in the past or proposed for the rainwater goods.

The main external walls to the main building are
faces of the external walls are fully rendered smooth with painted, pebble dash and mock
wall finishes. These are weathered commensurate to the age and type of property. These appear

The following damp data has not been generated and should be the first generated before all the damp related sentences in this section.

The walls have a barrier against dampness rising from the ground (called a damp-proof course or DPC) but were not visible because of (pointing/rendered plinth/other) but assumed to be (plastic/felt/slates/engineering bricks/other) for a property of this age.

Where accessible, moisture readings were taken with an electronic moisture meter at random points to internal faces of external walls, and no signs of dampness were observed. No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
Where accessible, moisture readings were taken with an electronic moisture meter at random points to internal faces of external walls, and signs of dampness were noted to the (type in location).
The below should both be captured and generated immediately after the wall type or types:
DPC: Where visible, the walls have a barrier against dampness rising from the ground (called a
damp-proof course or DPC) and assumed to consist of plastic, felt, slates and engineering bricks
You should remove the below from the damp sub section and add to the main section and should be captured and generated immediately after the above sentence:
DPC Treatment: I noted evidence of damp proof course treatment in the property. You should
ask your legal adviser to check whether there has been a dampness problem before and if a
valid guarantee or warranty covers this work (See Section I2 - Guarantees). If not, then, you
should ask an appropriately qualified person to inspect the property, assess its condition and
identify what repairs are required. Once the repairs have been completed, this person should
issue a certificate of ‘structural worthiness’. You should ask your insurance company if this
problem would affect your ability to get insurance cover for this property.
This default sentence has not been generated and should be generated first in this section:
Not every part of the windows
inspected in detail. The windows should be inspected as part of normal maintenance routine, and all moveable parts should be eased and suitably adjusted.

The windows installed in the property are made up mainly of replacement
sash, modern timber sash, aluminium and

units were noted, this confirms that the
units have been fitted with safety glasses. These appear in reasonable condition. No repair is
currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
The below should be one paragraph:
The window(s) installed in the property, lounge, bedroom and kitchen have damaged locks, are
difficult to open, are badly worn, are rotten, have broken panes, have failed glazing and are in
disrepair and are a safety hazard (See Section J3 - Risks). These should be repaired now as the
security of the property is compromised.
Windows that are in poor condition will require comprehensive repair. In some cases, it may be
economical to replace the windows entirely.
The design of the window(s) does not allow people to escape from a fire.
are in the lounge, bedroom and study; no opening, opening too small and no window at all.

is a safety hazard (see section J3 Risks). No repair is currently needed. The property must be

lock. You should replace or repair the door(s) soon.

Not every part of the conservatory or porch were inspected in detail. The conservatory or porch should be inspected as part of normal maintenance routine, and all moveable parts should be eased and suitably adjusted.

here as part of the first paragraph.

polycarbonate sheets, concrete tiles, clay tiles, mineral felt and lead.

units have been fitted with safety glasses.

by a contractor registered with FENSA. It is recommended that enquiries should be made

Not every part of the conservatory or porch were inspected in detail. The conservatory or porch should be inspected as part of normal maintenance routine, and all moveable parts should be eased and suitably adjusted.
There is a conservatory to the front of the property, and this is built of brick walls,
double glazed sections and timber double glazed sections. Visible British Standard Marks to the
units were noted, this confirms that the units have been fitted with safety glasses.
The roof over the conservatory is pitched and covered in
double glazed sections, polycarbonate sheets, concrete tiles, clay tiles, mineral felt and lead.
The conservatory incorporates single and double glazed
Also, the conservatory incorporates single and double glazed
The floors are covered in tiles, laminate flooring and carpets.
The property must be maintained in the normal way.
You should ask your legal adviser to check whether the
units have been installed by a contractor registered with FENSA. It is recommended that enquiries should be made regarding guarantees to the
The waterproofing at the junction of the roof covering and wall (called the flashing), appears to
be formed in lead, mortar and tiles. These appear in reasonable condition. No repair is currently
needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
The conservatory is open to the main part of the property, and this will result in a high rate of
heat loss from the property. A separating door should be fitted between them soon.
The conservatory is in poor condition. You should refurbish or replace it soon.
The same format applies to Porch.
The following sections are missing in

The usual external work at the eaves level (the edges of the roof where the guttering is fixed) could not be seen due to the height and configuration of the building. I cannot comment on their condition. These are normally formed in timber and should be maintained in good condition to prevent decay.
The usual external works at the eaves level (the edges of the roof where the guttering is fixed) include (fa
cias/soffits/bargeboards/verge clips/other) and these are made of (timber/PVC/Slates/other).
These appear in (reasonable/satisfactory) condition, no sign of significant defect noted. The property must be maintained in the normal way.

Because of possible asbestos content to parts of the usual work at the eaves level, the surfaces should not be drilled or disturbed without prior advice from an appropriately experienced or qualified person (See Section J3 - Risks). No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
required now, you must expect repairs in the future. When this occurs, you will have to use a contractor experienced in this type of work or an asbestos specialist.
E8: Parts of the usual work at the eaves level may contain some asbestos and is a safety hazard.

The (facias/soffits/barge boards/verge clips/timber cladding/other) to the (main building/back addition/extension/bay window/garage/other) are (rotted/damaged/poorly secured/incomplete/other). This should be repaired soon.
The above defect is a safety hazard. This should be repaired now.
Timber: You should plan to carry out some redecoration work to further protect the external timber works at the eaves level.
It is not possible to comment on the concealed sections of the
owever, a closer inspection may reveal some defects such as wood rot, e.g. behind guttering, soffits, and fascia.
by weathering and rot due to being exposed to the elements. Therefore, you
plan to repair and decorate exposed timber as required to reduce the risk of damage and decay.

External decorations help keep the property in satisfactory condition. Without a protective finish, roof timber elements will quickly deteriorate thus requiring extensive repairs. To prevent this, external surfaces should be redecorated periodically.
E8: Parts of the usual work at the eaves level is rotted. This should be repaired to avoid further deterioration.
Default text entry when FLAT is selected

As this is a leasehold property, the lease should state when external decorations are due to be carried out; however, you should ask your Legal Adviser to check that this is the case before the exchange of contracts.
You should ask the management company whether any repairs have been undertaken in the past or proposed.
Notes. Please type in your text in the box provided below…
This section is not applicable to the subject property, no further comments.
I was unable to inspect the communal areas, and so a condition rating has not been allocated. This was because (the area is not
comprise (automatic gates/CCTV/communal door/entry system/drive access/car park/ walk paths/ gardens/grounds/
Usual wear and tear were noted to masonry work but generally appear in (reasonable/satisfactory condition). Based upon visual inspection, common areas

I assume common parts are the subject of appropriate maintenance contracts and the responsibility of the management company. Your legal adviser should obtain full details. See Section I3 Issues for your legal advisers.

Your legal adviser should confirm that the person in control of the management of the building has complied with all statutory regulations regarding asbestos. See Section I1 Issues for your legal advisers.
There is a (carport canopy/ porch canopy/roof terrace/ balcony/Juliet balcony/ external stairs/other) to the
The (balcony/carport/roof terrace/staircase/other) walls are built of
The (balcony/roof terrace/other) drains are laid with (lead/mortar/bitumen/other) and appear well drained and unobstructed.
These appear in (reasonable/satisfactory). No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
The (pitched/flat) roof over the (carport/porch canopy/external stairs/
These appear in (reasonable/satisfactory). No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
items, and this can overload the structure, be a danger during stormy weather, and damage the surface of the balcony. These should be removed soon.
I could not tell if the glazing to the (balcony/roof terrace/other) screens is safety glass and this is a hazard (see section J3 Risks).
You should ask an appropriately qualified person to inspect the glass and carry out any repair or replacements.
E9: I could not tell if the glazing to the roof terrace screens
The (balcony/carport/roof terrace/staircase/other) walls are (cracked/damaged/eroded/other). This should be repaired soon.
The roof cover over the carport canopy (has missing tiles/has slipped tiles/ is in disrepair/ is leaking/ is dilapidated/has damaged flashing/is poorly secured). This should be repaired soon.
The surface of the roof terrace floor is (split/cracked/other). This should be repaired soon.
The roof terrace drains are (too small/ blocked/poorly drained) and cause rainwater ponding. This could allow dampness to get into the building and should be kept clear.
The surface of the (steps/landing) is (split/ cracked/partly rotted/rusted). This should be repaired soon.
The decorations to the (stairway/fire escape) are (perished/flaking/other). This should be repaired soon.
The handrail is (not strong enough/ inadequately designed) and could allow people to fall. You should repair or replace with a
more secured alternative (see Section J3 Risks).
(not strong enough/ partly rotten/ inadequately designed) and could allow people to fall. You should repair or replace with a more secured alternative (see Section J3 Risks).
paragraph for this section and should be generated

Inspection Limitations: Our inspection was limited to those parts of the property that were reasonably accessible at the time. We do not move heavy furniture, lift fitted floor coverings, or remove fixtures, fittings, or finishes. Areas that were covered, concealed or otherwise inaccessible were not inspected and no assurance can be given regarding their condition.

Where there is no suitable loft access hatch or due to obstructions, we would be unable to fully inspect the roof void. As a result, we could not assess the condition of the roof structure or confirm the presence, type, adequacy or condition of insulation within the roof space. Where available, the Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) should be reviewed alongside this report as it remains the primary document confirming the level of roof insulation.

Moisture and Dampness: Moisture readings and visual observations provide only an indication of conditions at the time of inspection. Such readings are not, on their own, a reliable means of diagnosing the source or cause of moisture within a building. Environmental conditions, including weather patterns, internal heating, ventilation, temperature and relative humidity, can influence the results obtained. Therefore, our observations at the time of inspection were inconclusive. For absolute certainty or for your peace of mind, we recommend further investigation by a suitably qualified and independent specialist experienced in building moisture assessment. The investigation should be undertaken in accordance with the Joint Position Statement 2022: Investigation of moisture and its effects on traditional buildings and should be carried out on an impartial basis, rather than by a contractor whose primary purpose is to provide quotations for remedial works. This investigation should be undertaken as soon as practicable and before exchange of contracts if you intend to proceed with the purchase.
The roof structure is built of traditional cut timber construction. The underside of the roof slope
or timber boarding installed. The absence of such undercover could
result in water leakage, lower internal temperatures, higher heating costs, condensation, and
I will recommend that you contact a qualified
person to inspect the roof covering to ascertain its water tightness. The property must be
No ventilation has been provided in the roof space. The roof space must be ventilated to the
outside air to prevent dampness. When installing insulation, the ventilation must not be
obstructed. The roof space does not have sufficient ventilation, and this has caused
condensation and mould growth in places (See Section J1 - Risks). Additional ventilation should
No ventilation has been provided in the roof space. The roof space must be ventilated to the outside air to prevent dampness. When installing insulation, the ventilation must not be obstructed.
The roof space does not have sufficient ventilation, and this has caused condensation and mould growth in places (See Section J1 - Risks). Additional ventilation should be provided now.
tank(s) within the roof space, airing cupboard and kitchen are formed in plastic,
galvanised metal and asbestos and appears in reasonable condition.
The water tank and accompanying pipe work appear to be adequately insulated, and no sign of
leakage or condensation was noted at the time of my inspection.
, damp, and leakages to generate the report.
The water and accompanying pipework are not insulated. I did not observe any condensation,
dampness or leakages to the ceiling or surrounding areas. The property must be maintained in
Because you have used repair header, you should
implement it globally for consistency: For example, here you have ‘Wall Repair
’, and ‘Repair Sealant’ but you have omitted ‘
Wall Repair’ to the below or it should have been generated
The original internal wall to the lounge, bedroom, kitchen and bathroom has been removed. The
walls and floors around the new or enlarged opening are distorted, cracked and poorly
supported. Long-term monitoring would, of course, be necessary to be certain regarding the
structural condition but is not considered warranted by my single inspection within the
You should ask your legal adviser to check whether the local council has granted building
regulation approval for this work and if a guarantee or warranty exists. If this does not exist, you
should ask an appropriately qualified person to investigate whether the building is properly
supported. Remedial work will involve removing parts of the floor and wall, and you should
discuss this with the current owner.
should be deleted from the phrase as it does not belong here
Some unevenness was noted, but this is within reasonable tolerances and does not appear to be
Some loose and creaking floorboard finishes were noted, and these will need repair or renewal.
When floor coverings are removed, other areas of disrepair may be revealed and require repair.
This as already mentioned should be
the end of each section and generated
, the sentence below should be generated
as part of the respective paragraphs:
These appear in satisfactory condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be
The fireplace in the lounge, reception(s), dining room, kitchen and bedroom(s) incorporates a
These appear in satisfactory condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be
Default text at the end of the
Kitchen sink, washing machine, dishwasher, etc. are considered plumbed in copper and plastic pipes, suitability of pipe connections and wastes are not readily determined.

Flexible sealants around the kitchen sink unit and worktop should be regularly checked and maintained as even slight damage may allow water penetration to enclosed areas beneath, which may cause rot and decay.
A representative sample of timber has been inspected, but the possibility of concealed defects being present in inaccessible timber cannot be ruled out completely.
Regular inspections of all moveable parts should be undertaken, and suitable easing and adjusting would be considered a normal maintenance feature.
The vertical part of each step of the staircase (called a riser) is open. Called an ‘open thread’ this
is a safety hazard, as small children could fall through the gap or become trapped. The staircase
The joinery items are made up of doors, architraves, stairs, stairs threads, handrails, balusters,
skirting boards and cladding, and these are formed in timber. These appear in reasonable
condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.
There are some cupboards of timber construction within the property. These appear in
reasonable condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the
The lock(s) to the lounge, bedroom, dining room and kitchen door(s) are damaged, in disrepair
and stuck. This should be repaired soon.
Default text at the end of the section not generated
You should regularly inspect, ease, and adjust all moveable parts of all wood fixtures as part of your normal maintenance routine.
A representative sample of timber has been inspected, but the possibility of concealed defects being present in inaccessible timber cannot be ruled out completely.
condition. No repair is currently needed. The property must be maintained in the normal way.

My opinion has been arrived at largely by the standard assumptions governing residential valuations. (These are outlined in this section and under market value on the description of the Home Buyer service). However, one additional assumption has been made: this is that the property has a Lease with an unexpired term of approximately 90 years.

The Lease still has a considerable term to run, and no action needs to be taken immediately. The value of leasehold properties will diminish as the lease gets progressively shorter. Once the lease is 80 years or less; it will have a significant impact on the valuation of the property, and you should, therefore, consider extending the lease under the terms of the Leasehold Reform, Housing and Urban Development Act 1993 well before this stage.
